attributes.stem:
A 25-year-old woman is to be admitted to hospital for an elective removal of a temporal lobe cavernoma. She is otherwise well and has no other medical conditions. Her only medication is carbamazepine, which she takes for seizure control related to her cavernoma.

On examination she appears well and vitals are within normal limits. Her body mass index is 24 kg/m2. Her renal function is 80 mL/min (60-120).

Consideration must be given to the risk of post-operative venous thromboembolism and the measures required to reduce this risk, starting from the time of admission.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate measure to reduce the risk of venous thromboembolism?
options: Graduated compression stockings and enoxaparin daily until discharge
attributes.explanation:
As this patient's mobility will be reduced in the immediate post-operative period, some form of thromboprophylaxis is important. The Caprini Score for venous thromboembolism is the most widely used and validated VTE risk assessment model for surgical patients. This score results in a risk stratification and also recommends which prophylactic measures should be implemented. The major factors involved in scoring system are:

  • Age (years): <40 (0 points), 41-60 (1), 61-74 (2), >74 (3)

  • Sex

  • Type of surgery: minor surgery <45 mins (1 point), major surgery (2), elective major lower extremity arthroplasty (3)

  • Events within the last month: range from 1 to 5 points and include things such as recent stroke, hip/pelvis/leg fractures and immobilising cast

  • Venous disease and clotting disorder

  • Mobility: normal (0), medical patient currently on bed rest (1), patient confined to bed >72hrs (2)

  • Other present or medical history: IBD (1), BMI >25 kg/m2 (1), AMI (1), COPD (1), Present/previous malignancy (2)

This patient scores 2 on this scale and is thus defined as being low risk for VTE. The current recommendations for these patients are to apply an intermittent pneumatic compression device on admission or well prior to surgery. This patient is young and should be able to mobilise on the first post-operative day. She should be admitted on the day of surgery, thus decreasing the time she is immobile in bed. She should have graded compression stockings on the day of surgery and the intermittent pneumatic compression stockings applied in or before theatre. There is a huge risk with any form of blood thinner and terrible consequences if she does bleed.

Enoxaparin is not indicated in this scenario. Two factors point away from chemical prophylaxis for this patient - first, the patient has baseline low risk for VTE by virtue of age and lack of comorbidities. Second, any increased risk of bleeding is of considerable concern after neurosurgery as intracranial haemorrhage carries a poor prognosis.

In a patient at higher risk of VTE chemoprophylaxis may be considered. A survey of neurosurgeons showed that some 27% of a cohort of 370 respondents used compression devices alone and of those who added some form of chemoprophylaxis, this was not started until 24 - 48 hours after the surgical procedure.

Graduated compression stockings provide reduced protection from VTE when the patient is immobile, and therefore are less appropriate if used alone. Intermittent pneumatic compression should be continued until the patient is mobile.
learningPoints: Local compression devices alone can be used for the young, fit patients at low risk of post-operative venous thromboembolism
Chemical prophylaxis for venous thromboembolism must be weighed against the risk of bleeding in high-risk procedures.
references: Wang Y, Huang D, Wang M, Liang Z. Can intermittent pneumatic compression reduce the incidence of venous thrombosis in critically ill patients: a systematic review and meta-analysis. Clin Appl Thromb Hemost. 2020;26:1076029620913942. Available from: https://doi.org/10.1177/1076029620913942

Faraoni D, Comes RF, Geerts W, et al. European guidelines on perioperative venous thromboembolism prophylaxis. Eur J Anaesthesiol. 2018;35(2):90–5. Available from: https://doi.org/10.1097/EJA.0000000000000763

Adeeb N, Hattab T, Savardekar A, et al. Venous thromboembolism prophylaxis in elective neurosurgery: a survey of board-certified neurosurgeons in the United States and updated literature review. World J Neurosurg. 2021;150:631–8. Available from: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.wneu.2021.03.049
Caprini JA. Caprini Score for Venous Thromboembolism (2005). MDCalc [Internet]. Available from: https://www.mdcalc.com/calc/3970/caprini-score-venous-thromboembolism-2005#evidence
